content: We know from fifteen references in (Acts 7)–13 that Paul was also called “Saul,” the Hb. name borne by Israel’s first king, who had belonged to the tribe of Benjamin (1 Sam. 9:1, 2), as did the apostle (Phil. 3:5). In his letters, however, he always calls himself “Paul,” a common Roman name, probably connected with his Roman citizenship (Acts 16:37, 38; 22:25–28). Before entering Galatia, Paul had evangelized the proconsul of Cyprus, who bore the same name (Sergius Paulus; Acts 13:9 note).

The word means “messenger.” Paul could on occasion use it to refer to an envoy sent by a church such as Epaphroditus (Phil. 2:25), but he uses it often in Galatians to denote emissaries uniquely sent by the risen Christ and bearing His delegated authority, including the original apostles at Jerusalem (1:17, 19; 1 Cor. 15:9). Paul here identifies himself as an apostle along these latter lines. Paul claims the authority of an apostle to lay the foundation of the church (1 Cor. 3:10; 9:1; 14:37, 38; Eph. 2:20; 3:3–5). His apostleship and calling came directly from God Himself (1:11–2:10). See note on (2 Cor 1:1).

Without denying Jesus’ humanity, Paul here implies His divine authority by identifying Him with the Father who raised Him from the dead. Christ directly calls His apostles, without human intermediaries (Luke 6:13; Acts 1:2, 21–26; 9:15). Apparently the Judaizers in Galatia sought to undermine Paul’s gospel by alleging that his authority was derived from the Twelve (vv. 11, 12, 16–20).
sections.section: not from men nor through man.

content: Each of Paul’s letters begins with a reference to these two blessings from God. “Grace” translates the Gk. charis, which means “an undeserved act of kindness.” Paul uses this word more often than any other NT writer and gives it immense theological significance. It refers to all that God has given us in Christ, nothing of which we have earned or can repay. “Peace” refers to the relationship that Christ’s death and resurrection (1:4) have established with God for those who believe the gospel. For Paul’s own comments on the meaning of these two terms, see Rom. 5:1, 2.
sections.section: GALATIANS 1:3 Grace to you and peace.

content: Only Christ, in His death and resurrection, can deliver the sinner from the order of united human rebellion against God. Believers no longer belong to this age but have been brought by Christ, through the Spirit, into “the age to come” (Eph. 1:21), which Paul will later refer to as the “new creation” (2 Cor. 5:17; Gal. 6:15).

This is not Paul the preacher, but God Himself (Rom. 1:6, 7; 8:28, 30; 1 Pet. 1:15). God’s grace comes to us at His initiative, by His call, and not because of anything we have done to deserve it (1:15; Rom. 4:4–8; 8:30; 9:11–13).
sections.section: GALATIANS 1:6 him who called you in the grace.

content: Though the Judaizers may present their message as “gospel,” no teaching that claims that Christ’s redemptive achievement needs to be supplemented by our efforts deserves to be called “good news.”
sections.section: GALATIANS 1:7 not that there is another.

content: Probably Jewish Christians from Jerusalem who insisted that Gentiles must not only believe in Jesus Christ but also accept circumcision and adhere to other aspects of the ceremonial law in order to become Jews and thereby be saved (2:3–5, 12; 5:4, 5; 6:12, 13). Various shades of this idea were widespread among some early Jewish Christians (Acts 15:1; 21:20, 21; Phil. 3:2, 3).

Those who add any additional requirements for salvation to faith in Jesus Christ, no matter how excellent their credentials, twist the gospel into another form. The preachers of the false gospel are under God’s condemnation.

Paul’s usual thanksgiving for his readers is here replaced by the threat of a covenant curse, repeated for emphasis. The Gk. word is anathema, used also in (Rom 9:3). Paul’s righteous indignation toward anyone who would obscure God’s grace in Christ comes to expression in his later wish that they would emasculate themselves (5:12), which would disqualify them from entering the Lord’s assembly (Deut. 23:1; see Phil. 3:2).

Paul’s opponents in Galatia attacked not only the gospel but also its messenger, Paul. One of their charges was that Paul preached an easy form of the gospel, requiring neither circumcision nor obedience to the Sabbath laws and dietary restrictions, but only faith in Jesus as Messiah (4:10; 5:11).
sections.section: GALATIANS 1:10 seeking the approval of man.

content: Paul defends himself against his opponents’ charge that he is in rebellion against the Jerusalem apostles who gave him his authority in the first place, or that his gospel is at odds with theirs. Paul contends that his authority and gospel come from God alone—the Jerusalem apostles merely confirmed those things. This argument extends at least through the end of chapter 1 and perhaps into chapter 2.

Paul’s persecution of the church before his call was well known to the early Christians (Acts 7:58; 8:3; 9:1, 2). Paul himself was profoundly ashamed of this part of his past (1 Cor. 15:9), although he looked on it as evidence that God’s grace can overcome the resistance of even the most rebellious sinners (Acts 22:4, 5; 26:9–11; 1 Cor. 15:10; Phil. 3:6; 1 Tim. 1:13, 14). Paul’s point in this and the following verse is that he did not derive his gospel from his pre-converted life in Judaism.
sections.section: GALATIANS 1:13 persecuted the church of God.

content: Paul is fond of pointing out to opponents like those in Galatia that being a Jew, even a zealous Jew, is insufficient for salvation. Paul considers his own experience proof that zeal for the law will not save (Rom. 9:30–10:4; 2 Cor. 11:22; Phil. 3:4–6).

Paul consciously echoes the call of Jeremiah (Jer. 1:5) and, perhaps, the servant spoken of by Isaiah (Is. 49:1, 5), whose prophecies spoke of the progress of the gospel to the Gentiles. Paul is conscious that his apostleship (v. 1 note) is in continuity with the OT prophetic tradition (see Rom. 15:8–12).

See note on v. 3; theological note “Effectual Calling”. Paul’s call to be an apostle, like the faith of every believer, is the product of God’s prior grace. Before birth, and therefore before they could do anything good or bad, God chose to create faith in His elect (Rom. 9:10–13; Eph. 1:4–6). No one can earn God’s call; it is a free gift.
